nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving to still blackwater streams, peat swamps, and forest-shaded pools with heavily stained, tannin-rich water over leaf litter and submerged wood; dense overhanging riparian vegetation

decorPreferences: dense planting with floating cover (Amazon frogbit, water lettuce, Indian almond leaves)
driftwood and twisted roots to create shaded midwater zones
dried leaf litter (Indian almond or oak leaves) on substrate to release tannins
dark or sandy substrate to lower contrast and reduce fish stress
peat filtration or blackwater extract to acidify and soften water
dim or diffused lighting with surface plant cover

diet.notes: Micropredator in the wild, feeding on small insects, zooplankton, and invertebrates. In captivity readily accepts quality micro-pellets, crushed flake, and frozen or live foods such as daphnia, baby brine shrimp, micro-worms, and bloodworms. A varied diet with regular live or frozen food promotes best colour and condition.

goodWith: other small blackwater rasboras (e.g. lambchop rasbora, harlequin rasbora)
small peaceful cyprinids from Southeast Asia
chocolate gourami and other small blackwater gouramis
pygmy corydoras and small loaches tolerant of low pH
dwarf puffer (with caution — size match required)
small peaceful tetras tolerant of soft acidic water
avoidWith: boisterous or fast-moving species that may outcompete at feeding

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species page image for Rasbora kalochroma — standard reference photograph showing adult lateral view with characteristic dual black spots.
Wikimedia Commons image; confirm license on retrieval. Good lateral shot showing adult coloration.

summary: A large, boldly marked Southeast Asian rasbora from Sumatran and Bornean peat swamps, distinguished by two prominent black spots on a rosy-pink body. Requires warm, very soft and acidic blackwater conditions; unsuitable for hard or alkaline setups. Less commonly traded than its care requirements suggest.
careNotes: The Clown Rasbora is one of the largest members of its genus, reaching 8–10 cm — considerably bigger than the common harlequin or lambchop rasbora. It originates from the peat swamp forests of Sumatra and Borneo (Peninsular Malaysia, Kalimantan, Sumatra, and Sarawak/Sabah), where water is dark with dissolved tannins and humic acids, extremely soft (gH near 0), and acidic (pH often 4.0–5.5). Replicating this blackwater chemistry is essential: fish kept in neutral or hard water tend to fade in colour, become stressed, and show increased susceptibility to disease. Peat filtration, RO water with blackwater extract, or the addition of dried Indian almond leaves and oak leaves are all appropriate approaches. Temperature should be maintained between 23–28°C. A tank of at least 120 L with a 100 cm footprint is recommended for a group. Subdued lighting, floating plants, and driftwood create the shaded, tannin-stained environment the species requires. The Clown Rasbora is loosely shoaling rather than tightly schooling — groups of 6+ move together in a relaxed manner but do not form the tight formations typical of danios or smaller rasboras. It is less commonly available in the trade than its appearance suggests; most specimens are wild-collected, making fragile transport and acclimation a concern. Newly imported fish should be quarantined and acclimated gradually to stable blackwater conditions before introduction to the display tank.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is rarely achieved and considered difficult. Like most rasboras, the Clown Rasbora is an egg-scatterer, broadcasting adhesive eggs among fine-leaved plants. Conditioning pairs on live foods, providing very soft acidic water (pH 4.5–5.5, near-zero hardness), and using a separate spawning tank with dense planting (java moss, hornwort, spawning mops) is recommended. Adults will predate eggs if not separated. Fry are very small and initially require infusoria or commercial fry food, graduating to baby brine shrimp. Successful captive breeding reports are infrequent in the hobby literature.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Rasbora kalochroma
primary.notes: Primary source. Species page provides native range (Sumatra, Borneo, Peninsular Malaysia), water parameters (pH 4.0–6.5, temperature 23–28°C, hardness < 5 dGH), adult size (to 10 cm), diet (invertebrates, insects, zooplankton), habitat description (blackwater peat swamps), and husbandry notes including group size and tank setup requirements.

additional.name: FishBase — Rasbora kalochroma
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 10 cm TL), native range (Malaysia, Indonesia, Brunei), taxonomy (Cyprinidae, Cypriniformes), temperature range (23–27°C), and habitat type. Confirms demersal/pelagic habitat in freshwater blackwater environments.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Rasbora Care Guide
additional.notes: General rasbora care overview referenced for feeding guidance, group size recommendations, and compatible tankmates. Clown Rasbora not profiled individually but general rasbora husbandry principles applied.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Rasbora kalochroma species profile
additional.notes: Trade availability context and notes on wild-collected status; confirms soft-acidic blackwater requirement, larger size compared to other rasboras (up to 10 cm), and characteristically loose shoaling behaviour. Recommended minimum group of 6.

additional.name: FishBase — Rasbora kalochroma distribution and ecology
additional.notes: Distribution and ecology data cross-reference confirming Indonesian records (Sumatra, Kalimantan) and Malaysian records (Peninsular Malaysia, Sarawak). Used to populate nativeRange.countries.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer; captive breeding rarely achieved. Requires very soft acidic water (pH 4.5–5.5, near-zero hardness), live food conditioning, and fine-leaved spawning medium. Adults must be removed after spawning. Fry are minute and require infusoria initially.
